Glossary About
Medieval Latin Scholasticism

Peter John Olivi

1248 – 1298

A Franciscan dissenter on soul, will, and the reflexivity of self-knowledge.

The safest way to reconstruct Peter John Olivi’s philosophy of mind is to resist two temptations: first, the temptation to treat him merely as a medieval theologian whose psychological claims are incidental; second, the opposite temptation to modernize him so aggressively that “mind” becomes a Cartesian or post-Cartesian category. Olivi is neither simply an Aristotelian faculty psychologist nor a proto-Cartesian. His central philosophical picture is closer to this: a mind is a living, active, self-present power that intentionally turns toward things, produces its own cognitive acts, and, at the rational level, possesses itself through free will. God and angels are not distractions from this picture. They are limit-cases and contrast-cases within the same metaphysical map: God as the simple, creative, exemplary intellect-will; angels as finite incorporeal intellectual subjects; human beings as embodied spiritual-rational subjects; animals as sensitive subjects lacking rational personhood.

Olivi, born around 1248 and dead in 1298, was a Franciscan whose most important philosophical work is his largely untranslated commentary on Book II of Peter Lombard’s Sentences, usually called the Summa quaestionum super Sententias. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy describes him as “one of the most original and provocative thinkers of the thirteenth century,” especially notable for his psychological theories and his voluntarist account of freedom; it also notes that his key philosophical discussions of metaphysics and human nature are concentrated in the Summa on Book II, edited by Bernard Jansen in the early twentieth century.

The following report reconstructs Olivi’s philosophy of mind as a system. It draws heavily on Anglophone scholarship, but also on French, Spanish, and Italian work, because non-Anglophone reception has been especially attentive to angelic subjectivity, divine ideas, self-knowledge, and the emergence of subjectivity in Olivi. Tiziana Suarez-Nani’s French study of angelic subjectivity, Ignacio Anchepe’s Spanish work on self-knowledge, and Martina Piantoni’s Italian work on Olivi’s doctrine of the soul are especially useful correctives to an overly narrow English-language account.

I. The central thesis: mind as active, intentional, reflexive, and free

Olivi’s philosophy of mind can be organized around four master claims.

First, cognition is not a passive reception of forms or species. The soul does not become cognitive because external things, species, or bodily organs efficiently produce its cognitive act. The soul itself is the active source of cognition.

Second, cognition is intrinsically intentional. A cognitive act is directed toward an object; the object is not normally an inner representation but the external or intended thing itself. The object “terminates” the act: it gives the act its objectual direction and content, but it does not efficiently cause the act.

Third, mindedness involves reflexivity. Even sensory life has limited reflexive structures, especially through touch and common sense. Intellectual life has stronger reflexivity: the soul is immediately aware that it exists, lives, cognizes, and wills. Rational self-awareness is not merely an inference from behavior or a later theoretical discovery.

Fourth, full personhood is completed in free will. The highest mental power is not bare intellect but a self-moving will. A rational creature is a person because it can possess itself, govern itself, and originate acts in a way that is not reducible to external causation, sensory appetite, or intellectual necessitation.

This makes Olivi’s philosophy of mind neither simple Augustinian illuminationism nor standard Aristotelian abstractionism. He is Augustinian in his emphasis on the soul’s inwardness, activity, self-presence, and will. But he is sharply critical of divine illumination as an account of ordinary human knowledge, and he moves surprisingly close to a kind of direct realism about cognition. He denies that the human intellect needs a direct infusion of divine light to make true judgments, because that would threaten both human cognitive agency and divine transcendence.

II. The ontology of soul: spiritual hylomorphism without Cartesian dualism

Olivi accepts a form of universal hylomorphism: every substance other than God is composed of form and matter. This includes not only corporeal substances but also spiritual creatures such as human souls and angels. In Olivi’s view, there is spiritual matter as well as corporeal matter, and spiritual beings are not pure forms in the strictest sense; only God is utterly without matter or composition.

This is crucial for his philosophy of mind. The human soul is not a detachable Cartesian thinking substance in the seventeenth-century sense. It is a spiritual substance with its own metaphysical structure, but it is also naturally ordered toward embodiment. Olivi famously denies that the rational part of the soul is, strictly and per se, the form of the body. The rational part is spiritual and incorporeal. The sensory and nutritive parts, by contrast, are the body’s forms more directly. Yet he does not deny that the whole human soul is in some sense the form of the body; rather, the rational part is united to the body through the sensory part and through the soul’s spiritual matter.

The result is a mediated hylomorphic dualism. Olivi wants three claims at once: the rational soul is genuinely spiritual; the human being is genuinely one substance; and embodied life is not an accidental costume worn by an already complete intellect. This is more dualist than Aquinas’s view, because Aquinas identifies the human intellectual soul as the substantial form of the body more directly. But it is less dualist than Descartes, because the soul is not primarily an isolated res cogitans accidentally joined to a machine. Olivi’s human mind is embodied, but its highest operations are not reducible to bodily organ-function.

The metaphysical reason is tied to his broader doctrine of “real aspects” or rationes reales. Olivi often resists multiplying distinct things where conceptual distinctions can be made within one reality. The mind can distinguish real aspects that are not merely fabricated by thought but also are not separate substances or really distinct entities in the strongest sense. This is important for his psychology because he frequently needs to distinguish powers, acts, orientations, aspects, and formal relations without turning each into an independent thing.

III. The faculty architecture: nutritive, sensitive, intellective, voluntary

Olivi works within the medieval faculty-psychological framework, but he reorders its significance. The soul has nutritive, sensitive, and intellective dimensions, and rational life includes intellect and will. Yet the decisive feature of soul is not passive receptivity but active power. He is repeatedly concerned to show that cognitive faculties are not inert capacities waiting to be activated by objects or species.

At the sensitive level, Olivi gives a special role to touch and common sense. He treats touch as unusually reflexive because the body is, in a broad sense, the organ of touch; through touch, an animal has a kind of bodily self-presence. He also reduces the traditional plurality of internal senses to common sense more radically than many scholastics. Common sense is the highest cognitive power of the animal soul and the only internal sense Olivi fully recognizes as an independent internal sense. It coordinates sensory life, enables awareness of sensing, and grounds a limited animal self-relation.

This means that Olivi’s philosophy of mind includes non-human animals, though not as rational persons. Animals have perceptual directedness, bodily self-awareness, memory-like and imagination-like functions, and practical orientation to their environment. They are not mere automata. But they do not have the full reflexive, intellectual, and volitional self-possession that constitutes personhood.

At the rational level, intellect and will become the decisive powers. The intellect knows not only external things but also itself and its acts. The will is the apex of reflexivity because it can move itself, suspend, choose, and direct attention and action. Olivi’s anthropology therefore culminates not in theoretical contemplation alone but in free personal agency.

IV. Perception: the active soul against passive reception

Olivi’s theory of perception is one of the most philosophically powerful parts of his psychology. He rejects the idea that perception is simply the passive reception of sensible species from external objects. In the standard Aristotelian model, as often received in the thirteenth century, an external object acts on the sensory organ through a sensible species; the sense power receives the form and thereby perceives. Olivi considers this too passive to explain actual cognition.

For Olivi, external objects and bodily changes are real and indispensable, but they are not the efficient causes of perceptual acts. A luminous object may affect the eye; the sun may damage the organ; bodily impressions may occur. But the bodily alteration is not yet perception. The cognitive act belongs primarily to the power of the soul, not to the organ as a merely physical patient. Silva and Toivanen summarize Olivi’s position as a challenge to the Aristotelian passive model: the soul causes its own cognitive acts, while the object provides the intentional term of those acts.

The key concept is aspectus, often translated as attention, gaze, orientation, or directedness. Cognition requires the soul’s attentive orientation toward an object. Olivi uses familiar phenomena to make the point: a sleeper or inattentive person may undergo bodily stimulation without having the corresponding conscious perception. Mere reception is not enough; the power must attend.

Olivi does not think the soul literally emits visual rays in the old extramissionist sense. He rejects a crude corporeal ray theory. But he borrows something from that tradition: the mind or sense power has an active “virtual” reach toward its object. The perceptual act is in the perceiver, but it is intentionally directed to the thing perceived. The soul’s power is virtually extended toward the object without physically leaving the body.

This yields a sophisticated account of intentionality. The object is not the efficient cause of the perceptual act, but it is the act’s terminative cause. The act is a seeing rather than a hearing because it is produced by the visual power; it is a seeing of a cat, for example, because the cat terminates the act and gives it its objectual content.

In modern terms, Olivi is trying to preserve three things at once: the object-directedness of perception, the active contribution of the subject, and the directness of perceptual contact with the world. He does not want perception to collapse into either brute physical affection or inner-picture inspection.

V. The anti-species argument: why representations can become veils

Olivi’s most famous epistemological move is his attack on species as intermediaries in cognition. He does not deny that there may be bodily traces, memory impressions, or images in certain contexts. What he rejects is the theory that cognition normally occurs by means of an internal species that functions as the immediate object of awareness.

His argument is direct and powerful. If the mind attends to the species, then the species, rather than the external thing, becomes what is known. If the mind does not attend to the species, then it is unclear how the species can explain cognition. A mediating representation either diverts cognition away from the object or becomes explanatorily idle. In the strongest version, species do not reveal the object; they veil it.

Olivi’s alternative is act-representational rather than object-representational. The cognitive act itself represents the object. There is no need to posit an additional internal item standing between mind and world. The act is intentionally structured; it is of or about the object by its own directedness.

This is why Olivi is often described as a direct realist. Yet one should be precise: he is not a naïve realist who denies all inner psychological mediation. He acknowledges bodily changes, memory traces, imaginative representations, and the need for sensory contact in ordinary cognition. His point is that the primary object of successful cognition is not an inner species but the thing itself. Cognitive mediation is functional and causal in certain respects, not objectual in the sense of placing a mental surrogate before the mind.

This also gives Olivi a distinctive place in the history of intentionality. His theory of attention anticipates later worries about representationalism: if consciousness is directed first to internal representations, how do we ever reach the world? Olivi’s answer is that intentional directedness is primitive. The act is not made intentional by a second object inside the mind; it is already a directed act.

VI. Attention and the problem of initial orientation

A subtle difficulty arises. If cognition requires attention, how does attention first find its object? Does one need to attend to something before one can attend to it? Would that generate a regress?

Olivi’s answer is that the soul has an indeterminate, standing attentiveness to its body and environment. Attention need not begin as sharply focused attention to a determinate object. There can be a more general field-like orientation, from which determinate acts emerge. Silva and Toivanen note that Olivi’s discussion of angelic cognition helps clarify this point: a subject may have a broad, indeterminate orientation that is later specified toward this or that object.

This is philosophically important. Olivi’s aspectus is not merely voluntary concentration in the modern sense. It is a structural directedness of living cognition. Focused attention is one mode of it, but the soul’s baseline orientation to body, world, and self is deeper than explicit concentration.

This is also where Olivi’s account becomes relevant to contemporary philosophy of mind. He distinguishes, in effect, between the physical stimulation of a sensory organ, the soul’s standing orientation, focal attention, and full conscious cognition. That distinction gives him resources for explaining inattentional blindness, peripheral awareness, bodily self-presence, and the difference between sensation as physical affection and perception as lived awareness.

VII. The mental word: concept, act, and theological analogy

Olivi’s theory of the mental word is another major site where theology and philosophy of mind are inseparable. His Tractatus de verbo arises from commentary on the Gospel of John and asks how the “word” in the human mind compares to the divine Word in God. But the analysis is not merely devotional. It forces him to ask what a concept is, how thought represents, and whether the mind’s “word” is an object, product, or act. Cambridge’s presentation of the text notes that the medieval debate over the mental word concerns concept formation and how the intellect represents the world under conceptual features.

Olivi’s mature answer is that the mental word is not a separate inner object produced by thought and then inspected by the intellect. The mental word is the very act of thinking. In other words, the mind’s word is not a little internal proposition, picture, or concept-object interposed between intellect and reality. It is the intellect’s actualized cognitive act.

The theological frame matters. The analogy with the divine Word pushes Olivi to think of intellectual expression as internal, active, and living, not as the manufacture of a detachable mental object. But the analogy also has limits. God’s Word is eternal, identical with the divine essence, and personally subsistent; the human mental word is finite, temporal, and act-like. The theological comparison therefore helps Olivi articulate the act-character of thought without collapsing human cognition into divine cognition.

This makes Olivi attractive to philosophers who distrust “concepts” understood as inner objects. He can say that thinking of humanity, redness, justice, or God is not first a confrontation with a concept-object called “humanity” or “redness.” It is an intellectual act directed under a certain formal aspect.

VIII. Abstract and propositional thought: the hardest problem

Olivi’s direct realism works most naturally for perception: I see the tree, not a species of the tree. But abstract and propositional thought are harder. What is the object of thought when I think “humanity,” “redness,” “being,” “justice,” or “every finite creature depends on God”? There may be no single external object terminating the act in the same way a visible cat terminates an act of seeing.

Here Olivi’s position is less complete. He rejects Platonism: universals are not separately existing objects. He also rejects strong object-conceptualism: concepts are not inner objects that replace external things as what we know. Yet he must still explain how abstract thought has determinate content. The SEP notes that Olivi’s direct realism faces a serious problem with abstract and propositional thought, and that his account leaves the status of abstract thought relatively unclear.

Han Thomas Adriaenssen’s study of Olivi and Peter Auriol is especially useful here. Adriaenssen emphasizes that Olivi and Auriol both reject indirect-realist accounts of cognition and stress the activity of the intellect, but he also notes that Olivi’s theory of conceptual thought is less straightforward than his theory of perception. Olivi wants cognitive representations to be acts rather than objects, but he sometimes makes concessions in the cases of imagination, memory, and conceptual thought.

Olivi’s doctrine of rationes reales may partly help. The mind can attend to real aspects of things without positing separately existing universals. For example, it can consider humanity, animality, rationality, or color as real aspects grounded in things, though not as independent things. But this does not solve every problem. Propositions, negations, modal claims, and theological truths require more than simple perceptual directedness.

A fair reconstruction would say this: Olivi has a powerful theory of perceptual intentionality and act-based conceptual representation, but he does not fully develop a general semantics for abstract and propositional thought. That is not a defect to be hidden; it is one of the key points where a contemporary “Olivean” philosophy of mind would need further development.

IX. Self-knowledge: immediate awareness without full self-comprehension

Olivi’s account of self-knowledge is one of the most original parts of his thought. He distinguishes immediate experiential self-awareness from deeper quiddative knowledge of the soul’s essence. The soul knows immediately that it exists, lives, cognizes, and wills; but knowing what the soul is by essence requires reflection, reasoning, and philosophical analysis.

This distinction is crucial. Olivi is not saying that every person has an innate philosophical theory of the soul. Rather, every conscious subject has a direct awareness of itself as existing and acting. I do not first infer from an observed act that some unknown subject must be thinking. I experience myself thinking, willing, seeing, loving, or attending.

Ignacio Anchepe’s Spanish study highlights the philosophical importance of this doctrine. He presents and comments on Olivi’s Impugnatio article 19 and Summa II, q. 76, both concerned with whether the soul knows itself by its essence or by a species. Anchepe notes that French scholars such as Olivier Boulnois and Alain de Libera have treated these texts as crucial for the history of subjectivity, even as we should be careful not to make Olivi simply modern.

Olivi’s self-knowledge is not mediated by an image. Against views that make the soul’s self-knowledge dependent on phantasms, Olivi insists that the soul can know itself without first forming an image of itself. The soul is not an external object for itself. Its self-awareness is more intimate: it knows itself as the subject from which its acts proceed.

Anchepe’s translation and commentary also draw attention to Olivi’s claim that, when we apprehend our acts, we distinguish the soul’s substance from its acts through an internal quasi-experimental sense: we perceive the acts as emanating from and depending on the soul, not the soul as emanating from the acts. That is a remarkably strong account of first-person subjectivity. The self is not merely a bundle of acts; acts are experienced as mine because they are experienced as proceeding from me.

X. Reflexivity and consciousness: the soul as self-present source

Recent scholarship has made Olivi especially important for the history of consciousness. Susan Brower-Toland argues that Olivi’s active and reflexive soul entails a kind of innate, perpetual self-knowing and helps explain the subjective character of conscious states. She carefully notes that medieval authors did not use modern terminology such as “phenomenal consciousness,” but that the phenomena under discussion overlap substantially with contemporary concerns.

On this interpretation, the soul’s self-presence is not merely dispositional. It has an “overflowing actuality”: the soul is structurally turned back on itself. But this permanent self-relation is not always focal consciousness. Olivi can distinguish between being explicitly aware of oneself and having a standing, peripheral self-presence that accompanies acts.

This gives him a powerful account of subjectivity. In ordinary perception, I am focally aware of the object: the tree, sound, color, person, or pain. But I am also peripherally aware of myself as the one seeing, hearing, thinking, or suffering. Brower-Toland emphasizes that, for Olivi, one never apprehends an act except as one’s own act: seeing is apprehended as my seeing, thinking as my thinking, loving as my loving.

This is also why Olivi’s anti-passivism matters for consciousness. If cognitive acts were efficiently produced in the soul by species or objects, then the subject would experience itself as a passive effect of external causes. But lived experience presents acts as issuing from the subject. The act is mine because it is brought about in my soul by my soul.

Brower-Toland argues that this allows Olivi to solve a problem left unresolved by more intermittent accounts of self-awareness, including Aquinas-like views where the intellect knows itself only by reflecting on its acts. If self-awareness arises only after an act has occurred, it becomes hard to explain the enduring subject of consciousness. Olivi’s stronger claim is that the soul is already self-present as the active source of its cognitive life.

A careful formulation would be: Olivi gives a theory of pre-reflective self-awareness, not in the exact vocabulary of phenomenology, but in substance. Consciousness is not a bare object-directed act plus a later inference to the self. It is object-directed awareness accompanied by a primitive, often peripheral awareness of oneself as subject and source.

XI. The will: apex of mind and root of personhood

Olivi’s philosophy of mind cannot be extracted from his theory of will. He is one of the great voluntarists of the later thirteenth century. But “voluntarism” should not be taken to mean irrationalism. For Olivi, the will is rational, reflexive, and self-moving. It is not a blind appetite but the power through which a rational subject possesses and governs itself.

His arguments for free will are deeply psychological. He appeals to the structure of moral and affective life: deliberation, remorse, praise, blame, obligation, self-command, and responsibility. If the will were necessitated by intellect, appetite, or external causes, these practices and experiences would become unintelligible. The SEP notes that Olivi thinks denying free will would reduce human beings to “intellectual beasts.”

The will, for Olivi, is undetermined in the sense that it is not necessitated by the intellect’s presentation of the good. It is self-determining. It can choose, refrain, redirect itself, and govern lower powers. This is why the will is the highest form of reflexivity: it does not merely know itself but moves itself.

Olivi even includes the ability to choose wrongly as part of created freedom. This is not because sin is good, but because a finite created will is not identical with God’s indefectible freedom. A created will’s capacity for self-determination includes the possibility of defect. This is central to his account of angels as well as humans.

Personhood follows from this. Olivi defines personhood in terms of per se existence, governance, freedom, and reflexive self-relation. A person is not just a thinking thing; a person is a self-aware, self-governing source of action. The SEP summarizes his account of personhood as requiring a mind with self-awareness and freedom of will.

This is one of the deepest differences between Olivi and many intellectualist accounts. For him, intellect is necessary for personhood, but intellect alone does not complete it. The person is fulfilled in free, reflexive agency.

XII. Divine concurrence and the autonomy of created mind

Olivi’s account of free will is linked to a striking theological-metaphysical position: he rejects the doctrine that God is the direct concurrent cause of every created action. He does not deny that creatures depend on God for existence. But he argues that natural and rational agents can produce their own effects without God directly co-causing each act.

This matters for philosophy of mind because it makes creaturely agency robust. The human will is not a mere occasion for divine action. The soul is a genuine source of its own acts. Olivi is especially worried about preserving responsibility for sin: if God were the direct concurrent cause of every act, then it would become difficult to explain why God is not implicated in sinful acts.

The point is not secular autonomy. Olivi remains fully theological: creatures depend on God for being, and God’s will is the ultimate source of creation. But within creation, God grants real causal powers. The created mind is not a fiction; it is a genuine active principle under God.

This is one of the reasons his philosophy of mind is so rich. He integrates metaphysical dependence on God with psychological and moral independence sufficient for responsibility.

XIII. Animal mind: sensitive subjectivity without personhood

Olivi’s theory is not limited to human intellectual consciousness. His treatment of sensory powers, touch, common sense, memory, imagination, and animal cognition implies a layered theory of mind.

Animals have perceptual awareness. Their sensitive powers are active. They are not passive receptacles of species any more than humans are. Through touch and common sense, they possess a limited bodily self-awareness. The SEP notes that Olivi treats touch and common sense as forms of reflexive awareness, though incomplete compared with intellectual self-awareness.

The key limitation is that animal reflexivity is semiplene, only partially complete. Animals can perceive their bodies and coordinate sensory acts, but they do not grasp themselves intellectually as enduring rational subjects. Nor do they possess the self-moving will that grounds moral personhood.

So Olivi’s hierarchy of mind looks something like this:

Animals are active sensitive subjects. They perceive, attend, remember, imagine, and respond purposively to their environment.

Humans are embodied intellectual-volitional subjects. They share sensitive life with animals but also have immediate intellectual self-awareness, abstract thought, and free will.

Angels are incorporeal intellectual-volitional subjects. They lack human embodiment but share the same basic intellectual and volitional structure of finite personhood.

God is the simple, infinite, creative intellect-will. He is not one mind among others but the ultimate source and exemplar of all created mind.

This hierarchy is not a ladder of mere “intelligence.” It is a hierarchy of reflexivity, freedom, embodiment, and causal sourcehood.

XIV. Angels: finite incorporeal minds as philosophical control cases

Olivi’s angelology is not a theological ornament. It is indispensable for understanding his philosophy of mind because angels test which features of human mindedness are essentially intellectual and which are tied to embodiment.

Tiziana Suarez-Nani’s French study, “Pierre de Jean Olivi et la subjectivité angélique,” is one of the most valuable treatments of this topic. She emphasizes that Olivi’s angelology concerns hylomorphic structure, relation to space and time, individuation, cognition, and comparison with the human soul.

For Olivi, angels are not pure forms. Like all creatures other than God, they have matter, though spiritual matter. They are individual, finite, located in some relation to space and time, and endowed with intellect and will. This means that angels are not miniature gods. They are finite subjects.

Their cognition follows the same anti-species logic as human cognition. Suarez-Nani shows that Olivi rejects innate and acquired species as the basis of angelic cognition and denies that the object efficiently causes the angel’s cognitive act. Angelic cognition is generated by the angelic intellect in the presence of the object, which terminates the act.

This is extraordinary. Many medieval theories make angelic cognition radically different from human cognition by assigning angels innate intelligible species. Olivi resists that move. He “humanizes” the angel in the sense that angelic and human intellectuality share the same nature and dynamism, though they differ by embodiment and by the conditions God has chosen for them.

Suarez-Nani’s interpretation is especially important: she argues that, for Olivi, the angel has the same type of subjectivity as the human being, minus corporeality. The angel has spiritual matter and form, individuality, active cognition, intentional relation to objects, and free will. Angelic subjectivity is not qualitatively superior in essence; it is a finite created subjectivity under different conditions.

This reinforces the systematic reconstruction. For Olivi, intellect and will do not absolutely require a body, because angels have intellect and will without corporeal embodiment. But human intellect is nevertheless naturally ordered to body through the human soul’s structure. The human mind is not accidentally embodied, even though its rational power is not reducible to body.

Angels also clarify Olivi’s theory of freedom. Angelic and human subjectivity share the same essential dignity because both are measured by liberty and moral self-determination. Suarez-Nani notes that the primacy of liberty makes the created realm homogeneous: rational creatures are united by free subjectivity.

XV. God’s mind: divine ideas, divine freedom, and the limit of analogy

Olivi’s remarks on God’s mind must be handled carefully. God is not a mind in the same genus as human and angelic minds. God is simple, infinite, uncreated, and immaterial without qualification. Since only God is exempt from matter and composition, divine cognition is not one case of spiritual hylomorphism.

Nevertheless, divine intellect and will are philosophically central to Olivi’s account of mind. The human mental word is analyzed partly in comparison with the divine Word. Divine ideas structure the relation between God’s knowledge, God’s will, and created intelligibility. Divine freedom also sets the ultimate contrast for created freedom.

The broader Franciscan tradition inherited Augustine’s doctrine that divine ideas are contained in God’s intelligence and do not differ from God’s essence. In the volume Divine Ideas in Franciscan Thought, Stève Bobillier’s chapter on Olivi argues that Olivi preserves the reality of divine ideas in God while interpreting them through his own concern for divine transcendence and freedom.

Bobillier’s account is especially useful because it connects Olivi’s critique of illumination with his doctrine of divine ideas. Olivi rejects theories of illumination that compromise human liberty or make divine transcendence too cognitively available to ordinary human knowing. Yet he does not deny divine ideas. He treats them as really present in God while not multiplying entities within the divine essence. They may be considered under the aspect of divine knowledge, as quiddities or intelligible reasons, and under the aspect of divine will, as ordered toward actual existence.

This means that God’s mind, for Olivi, is not merely contemplative. Divine knowing and divine willing belong together. God creates rationally, but not under necessitation. Creation is ordered by intelligible reasons, yet it is free. The relation between intellect and will in God thus illuminates the created mind: rationality and freedom are not enemies.

Olivi’s view of the beatific vision also matters. Bobillier notes that, for Olivi, the blessed directly perceive God’s essence and the plurality of divine ideas, but they do not comprehend God’s infinity. Even beatified minds remain finite. They can be directly related to God without exhausting God.

This is a theological claim, but it has philosophical significance. It preserves a distinction between direct cognition and comprehensive cognition. One can be directly aware of an object without grasping it exhaustively. That distinction fits Olivi’s broader direct realism: directness does not imply total comprehension.

XVI. Divine illumination: rejection without secularization

Olivi is one of the first major Franciscans to criticize divine illumination in a strong way. On illuminationist theories, human knowledge of truth requires some direct divine light or relation to divine reasons. Olivi worries that such theories weaken human intellectual agency and blur God’s transcendence. He therefore insists that human cognition must be able to make true and certain judgments through its own created powers.

But this is not secular rationalism. Olivi does not say that the human mind is independent of God in being. Rather, God creates the human mind with genuine powers. The human intellect can know because God has made it capable of knowing, not because God must directly illuminate each act as an immediate epistemic co-agent.

This is parallel to his rejection of divine concurrence. In both cases, Olivi distinguishes existential dependence from immediate operational dependence. Creatures depend on God for being, but their operations can be genuinely their own. The soul knows by its own intellectual act; the will chooses by its own free act; the perceptual power perceives by its own active orientation.

That is the theological foundation of Olivi’s philosophy of mind: the creature is most truly God’s creature when it has real created agency.

XVII. The embodied mind: body, organ, and colligantia

Olivi’s active-soul theory might look as if it makes the body irrelevant. It does not. He gives bodily organs, sensory impressions, and physiological changes an important role, but not the role of efficiently causing cognition in the spiritual power.

He uses the idea of viae colligantiae, ways of connection or linkage, to explain how bodily impressions and spiritual powers are coordinated. The body can be affected; organs can be disposed or damaged; sensory contact can occur. These bodily conditions are necessary for embodied perception. But the cognitive act itself is produced by the soul’s power.

This is why Olivi can say both that the soul is active and that perception is embodied. The eye matters for seeing, but the eye’s physical alteration is not yet visual consciousness. The ear matters for hearing, but sound’s effect on the organ is not yet auditory awareness. The body supplies the organic condition and sensory linkage; the soul supplies cognitive actuality.

The result is a kind of functional dualism without simple substance dualism. The spiritual and corporeal levels are functionally distinct, but the human being remains one living being. Toivanen’s book on Olivi’s perception and internal senses explicitly treats this under themes such as bodily changes, colligantia, and functional dualism.

XVIII. Memory, imagination, and absent objects

Olivi’s anti-species theory is strongest when applied to present perception. But cognition is not limited to present perception. We remember, imagine, dream, think abstractly, and consider absent things. Here Olivi allows a more complicated role for retained traces or species-like imprints.

The SEP notes that, while Olivi rejects species as intermediaries in direct cognition, he does allow representations in memory. Adriaenssen likewise notes that cognitive acts can leave memory species or imprints, and that Olivi makes concessions to indirect realism in imagination and conceptual thought.

This is not necessarily inconsistent. Olivi’s main target is not every internal representation whatsoever. His target is the claim that cognition primarily knows internal representations rather than objects. Memory images are useful precisely when the absent thing cannot be directly present to the relevant power. Anchepe’s discussion of Olivi’s philosophy of the image notes that an image is justified when it remedies the absence of the thing, provided one can distinguish image from reality.

So we should distinguish three cases.

In present perception, the object itself terminates the act, and no inner object is needed.

In memory and imagination, an internal trace or image may help the mind relate to an absent object, but the image should not be confused with the thing.

In abstract thought, the mind considers real aspects, universals, or propositions without positing separate Platonic objects or inner concept-objects, though Olivi’s full explanation remains underdeveloped.

XIX. Error, illusion, and hallucination

Any direct realist must explain error. Olivi’s resources are scattered but identifiable.

First, bodily organs can be improperly disposed. Since embodied perception depends on bodily linkages, defects in the organ can affect perceptual acts.

Second, attention can be misdirected, partial, or insufficient. Since cognition requires aspectus, faulty orientation can generate misperception or incomplete cognition.

Third, memory and imagination can substitute images for absent things. Images are legitimate when recognized as images, but they can mislead when treated as present realities.

Fourth, judgment can exceed sensory givenness. The mind may misjudge what is perceived, especially when it interprets, classifies, or reasons from perceptual acts.

Olivi’s theory is better at explaining error in perception and imagination than at giving a complete theory of propositional falsehood. That limitation follows from the broader difficulty of abstract and propositional thought. Still, the framework is not helpless: error arises not because perception is always mediated by inner objects, but because active cognition can be badly directed, badly linked to bodily conditions, or badly judged.

XX. Mind and moral psychology: virtues in the will

Olivi places moral psychology at the center of philosophy of mind. He holds that virtues are located in the will and that the moral agent’s internal intention is central.

This matters because it makes mind fundamentally practical, not merely theoretical. The highest form of rational mindedness is not the passive contemplation of truth but the free ordering of oneself toward goods. Attention, cognition, self-awareness, and will are interconnected. The will can direct attention; attention shapes cognition; cognition presents goods; the will chooses among them; and the subject experiences these acts as its own.

Olivi’s moral psychology is therefore a philosophy of agency. It explains why the mind is not just a knower but a responsible source of action. The person is a center of self-aware freedom.

XXI. Sleep, dreams, and impaired reflexivity

Olivi’s treatment of sleep and dreams helps clarify his theory of freedom and consciousness. He argues that choices made in sleep or dreamlike states are not fully free when reflexivity is hindered. The will’s freedom requires a certain level of self-possession, awareness, and control.

This is a subtle point. Olivi does not reduce freedom to indeterminacy alone. A random or uncontrolled act is not free merely because it is not externally determined. Freedom requires reflexive self-governance. The will must be able to possess itself, understand its act, and direct itself.

This helps distinguish Olivi’s voluntarism from arbitrariness. The free will is not a dice-throw inside the soul. It is a self-moving rational power whose acts are attributable to the person because the person is reflexively present to them.

XXII. Olivi and Aquinas: the deepest contrasts

Aquinas and Olivi share many assumptions: both are Christian Aristotelians in a broad sense; both accept faculty psychology; both treat the soul as form; both distinguish intellect and will; both take God, angels, and human souls as philosophically serious. But their differences are substantial.

On the soul-body relation, Aquinas identifies the intellectual soul directly as the substantial form of the body. Olivi denies that the rational part is per se the form of the body and instead mediates embodiment through the sensory part and spiritual matter.

On cognition, Aquinas accepts intelligible species as principles by which the intellect knows, while insisting that the species is not what is known but that by which the thing is known. Olivi worries that even this “by which” model risks turning cognition toward intermediaries and away from things.

On self-knowledge, Aquinas generally makes the intellect’s actual self-knowledge depend on its acts. Olivi gives the soul a stronger immediate self-presence. Brower-Toland’s comparison suggests that Olivi thinks Aquinas-like intermittent self-awareness cannot fully account for the enduring subjectivity of conscious experience.

On will, Aquinas gives intellect a stronger ordering role in choice; Olivi gives the will more radical self-determination. He insists that the will is not necessitated by reason’s presentation of the good.

On divine causality, Aquinas accepts divine concurrence; Olivi rejects direct divine concurrence in created acts, partly to protect free agency and divine innocence with respect to sin.

The contrast can be stated simply: Aquinas’s philosophy of mind is a hylomorphic intellectualism with strong metaphysical integration; Olivi’s is an active, reflexive, voluntarist psychology with stronger subjectivity and stronger creaturely agency.

XXIII. Non-Anglophone reception: what it adds

The Anglophone reception of Olivi has been especially strong on perception, direct realism, attention, species, and self-consciousness. Toivanen, Pasnau, Silva, Brower-Toland, and Adriaenssen are indispensable. But French, Spanish, and Italian scholarship often foregrounds different dimensions.

French scholarship has been particularly attentive to subjectivity, angelology, divine ideas, and Olivi’s place in the genealogy of modern subjectivity. Suarez-Nani’s work on angelic subjectivity shows that Olivi’s treatment of angels yields a serious theory of finite incorporeal subjectivity rather than a merely decorative theological doctrine. Bobillier’s work on divine ideas and beatific vision shows that Olivi’s account of God’s mind is linked to freedom, transcendence, and finite cognition.

Spanish scholarship, represented here by Anchepe, highlights the self-certifying or self-present character of the subject in Olivi’s texts on self-knowledge. Anchepe explicitly connects Olivi’s arguments to later debates about the subject while also situating them in their scholastic context.

Italian scholarship, represented here by Piantoni, approaches Olivi’s doctrine of the soul systematically: hylomorphic structure, plurality of forms, soul-body relation, sensitive and intellective faculties, intentional acts, and self-knowledge through reflection. This is useful because it prevents a narrow focus on only one fashionable theme, such as attention or direct realism.

A comprehensive philosophy of mind drawn from Olivi should combine all of these: the Anglophone focus on perception and intentionality; the French focus on subjectivity, angels, and divine ideas; the Spanish focus on self-knowledge; and the Italian focus on systematic soul doctrine.

XXIV. Systematic extraction: twelve Olivean theses in philosophy of mind

A comprehensive philosophy of mind extracted from Olivi can be expressed in twelve theses.

The first is the active-power thesis. A mind is not primarily a receptacle but a power that produces its own acts.

The second is the intentional-orientation thesis. Cognitive acts are intrinsically directed. This directedness is expressed by aspectus, attention, or intentional gaze.

The third is the terminative-object thesis. Objects determine cognitive acts as their terms, not normally as efficient causes. The soul causes the act; the object gives the act its objectual directedness.

The fourth is the anti-veil thesis. Inner representations must not be made the primary objects of cognition, because then they threaten to hide rather than disclose reality.

The fifth is the act-representation thesis. A cognitive act represents by being intentionally structured; it does not need a second internal object to make it representational.

The sixth is the embodied-linkage thesis. Human cognition is embodied through organs, bodily changes, and colligantia, but the spiritual cognitive act is not reducible to bodily alteration.

The seventh is the immediate-self-awareness thesis. The soul knows itself immediately as existing, living, cognizing, and willing, though it does not thereby possess a complete metaphysical definition of itself.

The eighth is the peripheral-subjectivity thesis. Object-directed awareness is accompanied by a non-focal awareness of oneself as the subject of the act.

The ninth is the self-moving-will thesis. The will is a reflexive power able to move itself, suspend action, and choose without being necessitated by intellect or appetite.

The tenth is the personhood thesis. A person is a self-aware, self-governing, free source of action, not merely a cognitive processor.

The eleventh is the finite-subject hierarchy thesis. Animal, human, and angelic minds are differentiated by degrees and modes of reflexivity, embodiment, intellect, and will.

The twelfth is the theological-limit thesis. God’s mind is not one finite mind among others but the simple, infinite source of intelligibility and freedom; nevertheless, divine intellect and will are indispensable for understanding the analogical structure of created minds.

XXV. Strengths of Olivi’s philosophy of mind

Olivi’s account has several striking strengths.

It gives a strong theory of attention. Perception is not merely stimulation; it requires directedness. This lets Olivi explain why the same bodily stimulus may or may not become conscious perception.

It avoids crude representationalism. By refusing to make inner species the primary objects of cognition, Olivi protects direct contact with the world.

It gives a robust account of subjectivity. The subject is not inferred from acts but is self-present in them.

It links consciousness and agency. The mind is not merely aware; it experiences itself as the source of its acts.

It includes animals without flattening the hierarchy of mind. Animals have genuine sensitive subjectivity, but rational persons have a stronger reflexive and voluntary structure.

It treats angels as philosophically informative. Angels show what finite incorporeal intellect and will would be like, and thereby clarify which features of human mind are essentially rational and which are embodied.

It integrates divine mind without reducing philosophy to theology. God’s intellect and will set the ultimate metaphysical frame, while human and angelic minds retain genuine created agency.

XXVI. Weaknesses and unresolved problems

Olivi’s theory also has serious difficulties.

The first is the mechanism of terminative causality. Saying that the soul efficiently causes the act while the object terminates it is elegant, but it leaves questions. How exactly does the object determine content without efficiently acting on the cognitive power? How does this differ from a merely verbal solution?

The second is abstract thought. Olivi’s act-based direct realism is powerful for perception, but less complete for universals, propositions, negation, modality, and theological concepts. Scholars such as Adriaenssen rightly treat conceptual thought as a pressure point in Olivi’s system.

The third is illusion and hallucination. Olivi has resources for error through bodily defect, misdirected attention, imagination, and judgment, but he does not provide a fully systematic theory of hallucination in the modern sense.

The fourth is soul-body unity. His denial that the rational part is per se the form of the body raises worries about whether the human being remains sufficiently unified. Olivi’s mediated solution is subtle, but it is not obviously more stable than Aquinas’s more direct hylomorphism.

The fifth is the status of nonconscious self-reflexivity. If the soul is perpetually self-present but not always focally conscious of itself, one needs a careful account of how non-focal self-presence differs from unconscious disposition. Brower-Toland’s reconstruction helps, but the underlying medieval texts still require philosophical development.

The sixth is the relation between divine freedom and created freedom. Olivi protects creaturely agency by denying direct divine concurrence, but this raises broader metaphysical questions about providence, causality, and the dependence of created operations on God.

These weaknesses do not make the project fail. They identify where an Olivean philosophy of mind would need systematic extension.

XXVII. A reconstructed Olivean model of mind

Putting the pieces together, one can reconstruct Olivi’s model as follows.

At the base, living beings are not inert mechanisms. A living soul is an active principle. In animals, this active principle is sensitive: it attends, perceives, coordinates bodily information, and has partial self-relation through touch and common sense. Animal consciousness is embodied, affective, and practical.

In human beings, the same embodied sensitive life is integrated with a spiritual rational soul. The rational part is not simply the body’s organic form, but it is united with the embodied soul through the sensory structure. Human cognition begins in embodied contact but is not reducible to bodily change. The human mind actively attends to objects, produces cognitive acts, and directly knows things without first knowing inner species.

The human intellect is also self-aware. It knows itself immediately as living, knowing, and willing. This self-awareness is not full metaphysical self-comprehension, but it is enough to ground first-person subjectivity. The subject is present in its acts as their source.

The will completes the person. Because the will can move itself, suspend action, and choose, the human being is not merely an intelligent animal but a morally responsible person. Freedom is not a decorative ethical addition to cognition; it is the highest expression of rational mindedness.

Angels show that finite mind need not be corporeal. They are spiritual hylomorphic subjects with intellect and will, active cognition, intentional directedness, and freedom. Their difference from humans is not that they possess a wholly alien kind of subjectivity, but that their finite intellectual subjectivity is not mediated by a corporeal body in the same way.

God is the infinite source and limit. Divine intellect and will are simple, creative, and exemplary. Divine ideas ground the intelligibility of creatures, but divine freedom prevents creation from being necessitated. The beatific vision shows that a finite mind may directly know God without comprehending God infinitely.

Thus the Olivean philosophy of mind is a metaphysics of active subjectivity. A mind is not a theater of internal images. It is a living, self-present power that reaches toward things, knows itself in knowing, and, at the rational level, freely governs its own acts.

XXVIII. Primary texts to read for a full reconstruction

The indispensable primary base is Olivi’s Summa quaestionum super Sententias, especially Book II. The most relevant questions include those on soul-body union, cognition, attention, species, self-knowledge, reflexive acts, and freedom. The SEP bibliography lists the Jansen edition of Book II and identifies key translated excerpts, including material on cognitive attention, the rejection of species, the mental word, and the claim that the human soul’s rational part is not per se the form of the body.

For the mental word, the key text is the Tractatus de verbo, which emerges from Olivi’s commentary on John and compares the word in the human mind with the Word in God.

For self-knowledge, the Impugnatio article 19 and Summa II, q. 76 are especially important; Anchepe’s Spanish article provides translation and commentary on both.

For angelic mind, the relevant questions include those on angelic cognition, angelic matter, individuation, and freedom; Suarez-Nani’s article gives a strong map of the issues.

For divine mind, one needs Olivi’s material on divine ideas, divine knowledge, divine will, and beatific vision, especially as discussed in Bobillier’s chapter on Olivi in the Franciscan divine ideas volume.

XXIX. Secondary literature map

For orientation, the Stanford Encyclopedia entry by Toivanen and Pasnau remains the best concise starting point, especially because it covers metaphysics, illumination, direct realism, soul-body union, attention, self-reflexivity, and free will.

For perception and attention, read Silva and Toivanen’s “The Active Nature of the Soul in Sense Perception: Robert Kilwardby and Peter Olivi,” and Toivanen’s monograph Perception and the Internal Senses: Peter of John Olivi on the Cognitive Powers of the Soul.

For self-consciousness and subjectivity, read Brower-Toland’s “Activity and Subjectivity: Olivi on the Soul and Self-Consciousness,” alongside Anchepe’s Spanish translations and commentary.

For conceptual thought and the limits of direct realism, read Adriaenssen on Olivi and Auriol.

For angelic subjectivity, read Suarez-Nani.

For divine ideas, divine freedom, and beatific vision, read Bobillier’s chapter in Divine Ideas in Franciscan Thought.

For a systematic Italian treatment of the soul, faculties, intentional acts, and self-knowledge, Piantoni’s work is a useful guide.

XXX. Final synthesis

Olivi’s philosophy of mind is best understood as an active, reflexive, voluntarist direct realism embedded in a Christian metaphysics of finite and infinite minds. Its central claim is that cognition and agency belong to the soul as a living source, not as a passive recipient of external forms. Perception requires attention; cognition is intentionally directed to things; mental words are acts rather than inner objects; self-awareness is immediate and pre-reflective; and personhood is completed by free will.

God and angels are essential to this system. Angels show what finite incorporeal intellectual subjectivity looks like, and Olivi’s refusal to give them a radically alien cognitive structure strengthens the unity of his theory of created minds. God, by contrast, is the infinite limit: simple, free, creative, and the source of divine ideas. Human minds are neither divine nor angelic, but they are genuine created sources of knowing and willing.

The resulting philosophy of mind is not merely historically interesting. It offers a serious alternative to both passive empiricism and inner-object representationalism. It treats consciousness as active, world-directed, self-involving, and morally charged. Its unfinished problems—especially abstraction, hallucination, and the exact mechanics of terminative causality—are real. But the underlying vision is powerful: to be minded is to be a self-present power in intentional relation to the world, and to be a person is to possess oneself in freedom.

PreviousHenry of Ghent NextWilliam of Ockham

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
